does: This paper introduces GUARD-IT, a training- and gradient-free method for machine unlearning that operates at inference time through gated activation redirection. Rather than modifying model weights via gradient-based updates, the approach steers internal activations during inference using input-dependent gating mechanisms that selectively apply different interventions based on the specific input. The method targets the removal of influence from a forget set while preserving general model performance, avoiding the computational expense, irreversibility, and quantization sensitivity of weight modification approaches. GUARD-IT addresses limitations of naive activation steering by introducing conditional logic that prevents unintended behavioral changes across the model's full input distribution.
connects: This work builds on the emerging paradigm of activation engineering and inference-time model steering, which has grown as an alternative to gradient-based parameter updates for controlling language model behavior. It extends prior work on machine unlearning that sought to approximate models retrained without specific data, previously achieved through costly fine-tuning and gradient-based methods. The paper responds to recent activation-based approaches to model editing and alignment that demonstrated steering is possible without weight changes, while addressing their failure modes. The approach connects to broader research on memorization in large language models and privacy-preserving machine learning, as well as work on quantization-aware methods that maintain performance across different model compression regimes.
enables: This work opens pathways for practical deployment of unlearning in resource-constrained settings, since the method operates post-hoc without retraining or weight modification, making it compatible with quantized and deployed models. It enables more flexible unlearning where interventions can be continuously updated without irreversible changes, potentially supporting iterative removal of multiple forget sets or real-time privacy compliance. The gated activation approach may inspire future work on input-conditional steering more broadly, with applications beyond unlearning to targeted behavior modification, safety interventions, and personalization. The inference-time perspective could facilitate exploration of how model internal representations encode specific knowledge and how selective interventions preserve capability while removing unwanted information.
